---
title: "Sense-Making Methodology — A Practitioner Guide to Brenda Dervin’s Work"
pagetitle: "Sense-Making Methodology — Practitioner Guide & Question Rewriter"
description: "A public study guide and working instrument for applying Brenda Dervin’s Sense-Making Methodology to UX research, analytics, interviews, and better research questions."
---

```{=html}
<div class="smm-callout" id="smm-callout">
  <img src="assets/svg/icons/callout-sprout.svg" width="16" height="16" alt="" aria-hidden="true">
  <span class="callout-new">New here?</span>
  <span>Read the <a href="bridge.html">Orientation ↗</a> for a one-minute map, or open <a href="theory.html">Theory ↗</a> for the full picture.</span>
  <button class="dismiss" onclick="this.parentElement.style.display='none'; localStorage.setItem('smm_callout_dismissed','1');" aria-label="Dismiss">✕</button>
</div>
<script>if (localStorage.getItem('smm_callout_dismissed')) document.getElementById('smm-callout').style.display = 'none';</script>

<div class="practice-wrap">
  <div class="practice-head">
    <div>
      <span class="practice-eye">§ Practice · The Instrument</span>
      <h1 class="practice-title">Question Rewriter</h1>
    </div>
    <div class="practice-help">Don't know what a "gap" is yet? <a href="theory.html">Read Theory →</a></div>
  </div>

  <div class="smm-callout-box smm-callout-box-practice-note">
    <strong>Practitioner note</strong>
    The rewriter is a practicum tool, not an academic authority. It applies this course's practitioner gap labels as tentative prompts for analysis. Dervin's own work should be treated as the source for formal terminology and citation.
  </div>

  <div class="rewriter-card">
    <div class="corner-stamp">
      <img src="assets/svg/icons/instrument-stamp.svg" width="11" height="11" alt="" aria-hidden="true">
      <span>· INSTRUMENT ·</span>
    </div>
    <div id="smm-rewriter-root"></div>
  </div>

  <!-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
  <!-- DEEP CONTENT: Active recall below the rewriter     -->
  <!-- ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

  <details class="plate plate-spaced-top">
    <summary><span class="plate-n">Plate II</span><span class="plate-title">Flashcards — test your recall</span></summary>
    <div class="plate-body">
      <p>Click any card to reveal the answer.</p>
      <div class="fc-grid" id="fc-container"></div>
      <script>
      (function() {
        var cards = [
          { q: "What is the root metaphor of SMM?", a: "A person moving through time-space encounters a gap and must construct a bridge to continue forward. Every element of SMM flows from this spatial-temporal image." },
          { q: "Define 'gap' in Dervin's framework.", a: "The discontinuity that interrupts forward movement — a question, confusion, barrier, dilemma. Not a failure; the engine of sense-making." },
          { q: "How does a 'bridge' differ from 'information'?", a: "A bridge is any resource constructed to cross a gap: ideas, memories, emotions, social connections, procedures, or information. Information is one bridge type among many." },
          { q: "What are the four S-G-B-O elements?", a: "Situation (context), Gap (discontinuity), Bridge (constructed crossing), Outcome/Use (what results). The framework is recursive — outcomes create new situations." },
          { q: "What is sense-unmaking?", a: "Dismantling existing sense structures. Required for unlearning, transformation, and critical thought. Not failure — a constitutive part of the process." },
          { q: "Why does SMM insist on specific moments?", a: "Because sense-making is always situated in a specific time-space context. General questions abstract away the situational ground that constitutes the sense-making." },
          { q: "What is neutral questioning?", a: "Questions avoiding presupposition of gap type, bridge type, or outcome type — letting respondents name their own experience rather than conforming to researcher categories." },
          { q: "Name four movement-state or practitioner gap labels used in this course.", a: "Decision, Barrier, Problematic, Spin-out, and Washout are grounded in Dervin's movement-state / stop framing. Role is retained here as an applied course extension for organisational, UX, and product work." },
          { q: "What is the MMTLI?", a: "Micro-Moment Time-Line Interview. Six steps: situate, step-through, probe gaps, probe bridges, probe outcomes, probe hurts." },
          { q: "How does SMM differ from the transmission model?", a: "Transmission: information exists independently as an object. SMM: information is constructed in the act of bridging — always relational, situational, never free-standing." },
          { q: "What does 'outcome' include beyond 'was it bridged'?", a: "Helps (forward movement), Hurts (movement blocked), New gaps (requiring more bridging), and Use types: deciding, acting, connecting, evaluating, feeling." },
          { q: "Why probe for hurts as well as helps?", a: "Bridges often fail or generate worse gaps. Asking only about successes produces asymmetric data that misrepresents how sense-making actually works." },
          { q: "What does 'universality of procedure, uniqueness of instance' mean?", a: "Everyone sense-makes (universal procedure), but the specific sense any person makes is unique to their situation, history, and moment." },
          { q: "Why is 'situation' constitutive?", a: "It determines what counts as a gap, what bridges are available, and what outcomes are possible. Not background — it is the operative ground of all sense-making." },
          { q: "What is the core design principle SMM implies?", a: "Design for gap-bridging, not message-sending. Ask 'what gaps does our audience have?' not 'what do we want to tell them?'" },
          { q: "How does sense-unmaking relate to power?", a: "Dominant groups impose sense structures. Marginalised groups must unmake imposed sense to survive and resist. Sense-unmaking is both cognitive and political." },
        ];
        var container = document.getElementById('fc-container');
        cards.forEach(function(card, i) {
          var fc = document.createElement('div');
          fc.className = 'fc';
          fc.innerHTML = '<div class="fc-inner"><div class="fc-front"><div><div class="fc-num">Card ' + String(i+1).padStart(2,'0') + '</div><div class="fc-q">' + card.q + '</div></div><div class="fc-hint">click to reveal →</div></div><div class="fc-back"><div class="fc-a">' + card.a + '</div><div class="fc-hint">← click to flip back</div></div></div>';
          fc.addEventListener('click', function() { fc.classList.toggle('flipped'); });
          container.appendChild(fc);
        });
      })();
      </script>
    </div>
  </details>

  <details class="plate">
    <summary><span class="plate-n">Plate III</span><span class="plate-title">Test questions</span></summary>
    <div class="plate-body">
      <p>Click each question to reveal the answer.</p>

      <details class="tq"><summary><div class="tq-num">1</div>A UX researcher says: "Users need information about pricing, features, and comparisons." What would Dervin critique and how would she reframe this?</summary><div class="tq-body">This replicates topic-based, transmission-model thinking. Dervin would critique: (1) "information needs" abstracts from situation; (2) it presupposes information as the only bridge; (3) it describes content rather than the situated discontinuity. SMM reframe: conduct MMTLI interviews about specific moments — identify the participant's gap in their own terms, map bridge attempts (including non-informational ones), and analyse outcomes. "Pricing information need" may become: "a decision-oriented discontinuity requiring comparison to choose between plans — bridges attempted: pricing page scan (hurt: confusing), social query (helped), support call (mixed)."</div></details>

      <details class="tq"><summary><div class="tq-num">2</div>Explain why 'outcome' in SMM is more analytically powerful than 'satisfaction' or 'usability'.</summary><div class="tq-body">Satisfaction/usability measure reaction to an artifact in isolation. SMM outcome is tied to the specific gap: (1) Helps — what movement was enabled; (2) Hurts — how the bridge failed or worsened the situation; (3) New gaps — secondary discontinuities created; (4) Use types — what the person actually did differently. A highly "satisfying" document may bridge no actual gaps; a frustrating search experience may ultimately help enormously. SMM makes effectiveness situated and causal, not preferential.</div></details>

      <details class="tq"><summary><div class="tq-num">3</div>A communications director says: "We sent detailed strategy communications but employees are still confused." Apply SMM to diagnose this.</summary><div class="tq-body">The director applied a transmission model: encode → send → assume comprehension. SMM diagnosis: employees' actual gaps (What does this mean for my role? / Why is this better than before? / Who can I trust?) were never diagnosed. The communication was designed around what leadership wanted to transmit, not around the situated discontinuities employees actually have. Fix: MMTLI-style interviews asking about specific moments of uncertainty. Identify the stops or gap pictures that emerge. Design communications as bridges for those gaps — organised by discontinuity, not by leadership's topic preferences.</div></details>

      <details class="tq"><summary><div class="tq-num">4</div>What problem does SMM's sense-unmaking concept create for knowledge management systems?</summary><div class="tq-body">KM systems assume knowledge is a capturable, retrievable object — a transmission model. Sense-unmaking reveals: (1) Knowledge is a bridge constructed for a specific gap in a specific situation — a document that bridges one gap may fail entirely in another context; (2) Transformation requires dismantling existing "knowledge," which a deposit-and-retrieve system cannot facilitate. SMM-informed KM would index resources by the situations and gaps they have helped with, surface assumption-challenging materials, and build processes for questioning existing organisational sense.</div></details>

      <details class="tq"><summary><div class="tq-num">5</div>Design an SMM study of how expats navigate healthcare in a new country.</summary><div class="tq-body">MMTLI interviews anchored in a specific recent healthcare-seeking moment. Step through from initial need to resolution. Gap probes at each step: possible role-oriented gaps (how to register?), decision-oriented gaps (which specialist?), barrier gaps (language, system structure), trust gaps (who is reliable here?). Bridge analysis: internet searches, expat communities, calling home, GP visits, trial and error. Output: a gap map organised by situated discontinuities and bridge attempts, enabling communications and services to bridge actual gaps — not assumed "information needs."</div></details>
    </div>
  </details>

  <details class="plate">
    <summary><span class="plate-n">Plate IV</span><span class="plate-title">Key principles and common mistakes</span></summary>
    <div class="plate-body">
      <div class="smm-callout-box">
        <strong>Seven principles</strong>
        Study needs as situationally constructed gaps or questions, not static topic categories. Study <em>movement</em>, not static states. Study <em>specific episodes</em>, not abstract patterns. Study <em>bridges</em> (any type), not content delivery. Study <em>outcomes in both directions</em> — helps and hurts. Always situate: specific moment, specific person, specific context. Design for gap-bridging, not message-sending.
      </div>

      <div class="smm-callout-box warn"><strong>Equating bridge with information</strong> Information is one bridge type among many. Bridges can also be emotional, social, procedural, or drawn from memory. Privileging information replicates the transmission model inside SMM's frame.</div>

      <div class="smm-callout-box warn"><strong>Abstracting from situation</strong> Asking "what do users generally need?" rather than "what did this person need in this moment?" evacuates the methodology's explanatory power.</div>

      <div class="smm-callout-box warn"><strong>Ignoring sense-unmaking</strong> Treating SMM as purely constructive misses resistance, confusion, and transformative learning — contexts where old sense must be dismantled before new sense can be made.</div>

      <div class="smm-callout-box warn"><strong>Only probing positive outcomes</strong> Asking only "what helped?" misses bridge failures and hurts — often the most diagnostically valuable data.</div>
    </div>
  </details>

</div>

<script src="js/smm-data.js"></script>
<script src="js/smm-rewriter.js"></script>
<script>window.initSMMRewriter('smm-rewriter-root');</script>
```
